entries.back: An online platform or marketplace to buy, sell, and store cryptocurrencies. They allow trading between cryptocurrencies and often between cryptocurrency and fiat currency. They are often referred to as the 'banks' of the cryptocurrency space.

entries.front: What are the two types of cryptocurrency exchanges?
entries.back: Centralised exchanges (CEX) and Decentralised exchanges (DEX).

A Japanese bitcoin exchange that operated between 2010 and 2014. By early 2014 it handled over 70% of all bitcoin transactions. It operated without regulatory oversight and collapsed in February 2014 due to security breaches, theft, DDoS attacks, and internal fraud.

entries.front: What are the four general goals of cryptocurrency regulation?
entries.back: 1. Curbing Illicit Activity (money laundering, terrorist financing, tax evasion, financial crime). 2. Investor/Consumer Protection (market integrity, accountability, transparency). 3. Financial Stability. 4. Standardisation in the field to reduce risk.

entries.front: What is the 'Wait and See' regulatory approach?
entries.back: An early regulatory stance where authorities did not act on cryptocurrency, mostly because they had not heard of or were not affected by Bitcoin. It was largely unintentional in the early days.

A total ban or prohibition imposed on certain cryptocurrency activities such as mining. Algeria is an example, imposing a total ban on cryptocurrency activities per Article 117 of the Algerian Financial Law.
An approach where regulators actively regulate some aspects, activities, and legal persons involved in the cryptocurrency ecosystem.
Anti-Money Laundering — regulatory policies designed to prevent the use of cryptocurrencies or other financial systems to disguise illegally obtained funds.
Countering the Finance of Terrorism — regulatory measures aimed at preventing cryptocurrencies or financial systems from being used to fund terrorist activities.

Financial Action Task Force — the global financial watchdog responsible for setting international standards (Recommendations) to combat money laundering and terrorist financing, including for virtual assets.
FATF's term essentially equivalent to cryptocurrencies. Incorporated into FATF Recommendations in 2019.

entries.front: Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs) — FATF terminology
entries.back: FATF's term for cryptocurrency exchanges and similar intermediaries. Defined in 2019 as entities providing services such as exchange between VAs and fiat, transfer of VAs, safekeeping of VAs, and related financial services.

Requires VASPs to: monitor transactions; collect and hold user information for both originator and beneficiary; share this information with other VASPs and Financial Intelligence Units (FIUs). For transfers exceeding 1,000 USD/EUR, VASPs must collect name, account number, address, country, date/place of birth, and official government ID.

The first piece of EU legislation to incorporate cryptocurrencies. Adopted in 2018, transposed into national law by 2020. It incorporated FATF Recommendations, defined Virtual Currencies and Custodian Wallet Providers, imposed KYC/CDD requirements on exchanges, and granted FIUs authority to obtain addresses and identities of owners. It only targets crypto-to-fiat transactions, not crypto-to-crypto.
Adopted in May 2024, it updates AMLD5 with more compliance guidance, enhanced verification procedures and transaction monitoring, and increased cooperation and information sharing with FIUs.

A popular darknet marketplace that used Bitcoin as its payment method. Its founder, Ross Ulbricht, was caught in October 2013 and sentenced to life in prison in May 2015. Its shutdown prompted an enhanced AML/CFT regulatory focus.

Published in the Official Journal of the EU on 9 June 2023. Introduces the world's first comprehensive legal framework specifically for crypto-assets. It harmonises regulatory requirements across the EU and establishes specific standards for cryptocurrencies rather than adapting existing financial standards.

MiCA's term for cryptocurrency exchanges. Defined as a legal person whose business is the provision of one or more crypto-asset services to clients on a professional basis. Services include custody, operation of trading platforms, exchange, transfer, execution of orders, placing, reception/transmission of orders, and advice/portfolio management.

Must be: run by legal persons (corporations); have a registered office in an EU member state; authorised by the national competent authority (90-day review); use passporting (one registration covers all EU); non-EU CASPs marketing to EU clients are also subject to MiCA.

Act honestly, fairly, and professionally (Art. 66); Prudential safeguards like insurance and capital requirements (Art. 67); Safekeeping of clients' funds with fund segregation (Art. 70); Governance arrangements (Art. 68, 70); Complaint handling procedure (Art. 71); Conflict of interest management (Art. 72); Outsourcing rules (Art. 73).

A digital representation of a value or of a right that is able to be transferred and stored electronically using distributed ledger technology or similar technology. The definition is intentionally broad to be future-proof.

A type of crypto-asset that purports to maintain a stable value by referencing another value or right or a combination thereof, including one or more official currencies (e.g., PAX Gold, XAUt). Can be pegged to one or more official currencies and/or other values like precious metals.

A type of crypto-asset that purports to maintain a stable value by referencing the value of one official currency (e.g., USDT, USDC). Can only be pegged to one official fiat currency. Issuers must be authorised as a credit institution or electronic money institution.
A type of crypto-asset that is only intended to provide access to a good or service supplied by its issuer (e.g., Binance BNB, Cardano ADA, Brave BAT).

Issuer must: 1. Be a corporation. 2. Draft a whitepaper (technology, rights/obligations, risks, environmental impact). 3. Notify whitepaper to authorities before publication. 4. Make whitepaper public. 5. Have and publish a marketing communication. 6. Act honestly, fairly, and professionally. 7. Maintain security systems to Union standards.

Retail holders have 14 days from agreeing to buy to withdraw their agreement to purchase crypto-assets — with no fees, costs, or reason required. All payments must be reimbursed no later than 14 days from the decision to withdraw.

entries.front: MiCA — CASP Liability for Security Breaches (Article 75(8))
entries.back: CASPs providing custody shall be liable to clients for the loss of any crypto-assets resulting from an incident attributable to them. Liability is capped at the market value of the lost asset at the time of the loss. Incidents not attributable to the CASP include events independent of their operations (e.g., inherent DLT problems they don't control).

entries.front: MiCA — What does it NOT apply to?
entries.back: MiCA does not apply to: NFTs (Non-Fungible Tokens), DeFi (Decentralised Finance), CBDCs (Central Bank Digital Currencies), and Security Tokens (which are considered financial instruments under EU securities law — MiFiD2).
The EU regulatory package that contains two main components: the Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (MiCA) and the Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA).
A single CASP registration in one EU member state is sufficient to provide crypto-asset services across the entire European Union.

A problem arising from the FATF Travel Rule where VASPs must share user information with other VASPs that may not yet be compliant with regulations. This creates major security and data privacy risks.

A problem with early AML/CFT policies where, because regulations were not global, cryptocurrency exchanges could easily relocate to jurisdictions with more favourable or lenient regulations to circumvent rules they did not like.
A key AML/CFT requirement where VASPs/CASPs must verify the identity of their customers and assess the risk they pose. Required for transactions exceeding 1,000 USD/EUR under FATF Recommendations.
A process mandated by AML/CFT regulations (including AMLD5 and MiCA) requiring exchanges to verify the identity of their clients before providing services, as part of customer due diligence.

National agencies that receive, analyse, and disseminate financial intelligence to combat money laundering and terrorist financing. Under AMLD5, FIUs were granted authority to obtain addresses and identities of cryptocurrency owners. Under the Travel Rule, VASPs must share user information with FIUs.

FTX, one of the largest cryptocurrency exchanges, collapsed and filed for bankruptcy in November 2022 due to risky financial investments, misappropriation of customer funds, and security breaches. Founder Samuel Bankman-Fried (SBF) was convicted on seven charges of fraud and conspiracy and sentenced to 25 years in prison in March 2024.

Binance and CEO Changpeng Zhao ('CZ') pleaded guilty to federal charges in November 2023. Binance paid over $4 billion (the largest financial penalty imposed by US Treasury). They admitted to failing to maintain an effective AML programme, conducting an unlicensed money transmitting business, and violating sanctions law. CZ paid $50 million and served 4 months in prison.

entries.front: Hot Wallet vs. Cold Wallet (FTX context)
entries.back: Hot wallets are internet-connected and highly susceptible to hacking. Cold wallets are not connected to the internet and are more secure. FTX, despite reassuring users, stored the majority of customer funds in hot wallets, which contributed to its security failures.

To ensure EMT stability: initial capital of at least EUR 350,000 must be allocated at authorisation; at least 30% of funds received must always be deposited in segregated accounts in credit institutions; investments must be made in low-risk financial instruments.

Research found: 45% of exchanges collapsed before 2013; 50% closed before 2015; 40% closed before end of 2022; and in 2024 there were over $2.2 billion lost across 303 hacks (Chainalysis CryptoCrime Report 2024).

entries.front: Academic Study — 'Raising the Bar' (Academic Example 2) — Key Finding
entries.back: A study of 75 centralised exchanges in Europe found only 8% satisfied above 40% of MiCA/DORA compliance criteria. Key gaps: self-regulation deficiencies, custodial duty gaps, weak security policies, and liability shifting to users.

entries.question: What percentage of all Bitcoin transactions did Mt. Gox handle by early 2014?
Which country established one of the first comprehensive legal and regulatory frameworks for cryptocurrency exchanges following the Mt. Gox collapse?
Mt. Gox operated under proper regulatory oversight during its years of operation.
The Mt. Gox collapse created an unprecedented legal situation with no prior court cases and no proper regulatory user protection framework.
What were the four causes identified for the collapse of Mt. Gox?
entries.answer: Security breaches, theft, DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service) attacks, and internal fraud.

entries.question: What were the two main regulatory repercussions triggered by the Silk Road shutdown and Mt. Gox collapse?
options: A complete global ban on Bitcoin and creation of CBDCs
Enhanced AML focus and Japan's comprehensive exchange regulatory framework / global regulatory attention
Creation of MiCA and the Digital Financial Package
FATF incorporating VAs and the EU adopting AMLD5

entries.question: Silk Road used Ethereum as its primary method of payment.
Which of the following is NOT one of the three main regulatory approaches to cryptocurrency identified in the lecture?
Which country is cited as an example of the 'Hostile' regulatory approach to cryptocurrency?
Why did early regulatory efforts before 2023 primarily focus on AML/CFT policies for cryptocurrency exchanges?
entries.answer: There were three main reasons: (1) the obvious need to fight money laundering and terrorist financing; (2) AML/CFT policies could easily target intermediaries like exchanges as identified third parties; and (3) international and local standards already existed for traditional financial institutions, making it logical and easier to adapt them to cryptocurrency.

entries.question: How many FATF Recommendations currently exist?
FATF Recommendations are legally binding on all member countries.
Which of the following information is NOT required to be collected by VASPs under the FATF Travel Rule for transfers exceeding the threshold?
The FATF Travel Rule was welcomed without controversy by the cryptocurrency community.
What is the 'Sunrise Issue' in the context of the FATF Travel Rule?
entries.answer: The Sunrise Issue refers to the problem that arises when the Travel Rule requires VASPs to share sensitive user information with other VASPs that have not yet achieved compliance with the regulation. This creates major security and data privacy risks because compliant exchanges must share data with potentially insecure or non-compliant counterparts.

entries.question: AMLD5 covers both cryptocurrency-to-fiat AND cryptocurrency-to-cryptocurrency transactions.
Which EU directive was the FIRST piece of EU legislation to incorporate cryptocurrencies?
What were the five key issues identified with early AML/CFT regulatory policies applied to cryptocurrencies?
entries.answer: 1. Lack of consistency and unification in terminology and definitions, making compliance harder. 2. Regulatory shopping — exchanges could relocate to avoid regulations. 3. Inefficiency — policies were not targeted and applied blanket rules from traditional finance without regard for crypto-specific risks. 4. Enforcement issues and low compliance rates. 5. The Sunrise Issue with the Travel Rule creating security and data privacy problems. 6. Main focus on AML/CFT leaving out other important areas like consumer protection.

entries.question: What was Binance's CEO CZ's prison sentence?
options: No prison sentence, only a fine

entries.question: Binance pleaded guilty to failing to maintain an effective AML programme, conducting an unlicensed money transmitting business, and violating sanctions law.
What was the key security failure revealed about FTX during its bankruptcy proceedings?
entries.answer: It was revealed that FTX had stored the majority of customer funds in 'hot wallets' (internet-connected wallets), which are highly susceptible to hacking, theft, misappropriation, and inadvertent loss. This was despite FTX having always reassured users that it used the ideal hot/cold wallet combination. The industry best practice is to keep the vast majority of assets in cold wallets (offline) and only a small amount in hot wallets for daily operations.

entries.question: Samuel Bankman-Fried (SBF), founder of FTX, was sentenced to how many years in prison?
MiCA establishes the world's first comprehensive legal framework specifically for crypto-assets.
MiCA primarily adapts existing financial standards from traditional finance to apply to cryptocurrencies.
Under MiCA, how many days does a national competent authority have to process a CASP authorisation application?
Explain the 'passporting principle' under MiCA and why it is significant.
entries.answer: The passporting principle means that a CASP (crypto-asset service provider) only needs to obtain authorisation in one EU member state to legally provide crypto-asset services across the entire European Union. This is significant because it prevents regulatory fragmentation, reduces compliance costs for businesses, and creates a unified single market for crypto-asset services within the EU.

entries.question: Under MiCA Article 75(8), CASP liability for lost crypto-assets is capped at:
options: A fixed maximum of EUR 1 million
The market value of the lost crypto-asset at the time of the loss
The original purchase price of the lost crypto-asset
50% of the market value at the time of the claim

entries.question: Under MiCA, what qualifies as an incident 'not attributable' to a CASP, and why is this distinction controversial?
entries.answer: Incidents not attributable to a CASP include events that occurred independently of the provision of the service or the CASP's operations, such as a problem inherent in the operation of the distributed ledger that the CASP does not control. This distinction is controversial because exchanges are historically very quick to claim that incidents were not attributable to them, potentially shifting loss onto users. The challenge is proving attribution in a decentralised technical environment.

entries.question: Which of the following is NOT covered by MiCA?
Under MiCA, DeFi (Decentralised Finance) protocols are subject to full regulatory requirements as crypto-asset service providers.
Security tokens (considered financial instruments) are regulated under MiCA.
Under MiCA, an Electronic Money Token (EMT) can be pegged to:
Under MiCA, an Asset-Referenced Token (ART) differs from an EMT in that it:
options: Can only be pegged to one official currency
Can be pegged to one or more official currencies and/or other values or rights
Is not subject to reserve requirements

entries.question: Which of the following is an example of an Electronic Money Token (EMT)?
Which of the following is an example of a Utility Token?
Under MiCA, what minimum initial capital must be allocated by an EMT issuer at the time of authorisation to ensure stability?
Under MiCA's EMT reserve requirements, what minimum percentage of funds received must be deposited in segregated accounts in credit institutions?
Under MiCA, an EMT issuer must be authorised either as a credit institution or an electronic money institution.
What are the six key requirements for issuing an E-money Token (EMT) under MiCA?
entries.answer: 1. Be specifically authorised to issue (as a credit institution or electronic money institution). 2. Draft, notify, and publish a whitepaper. 3. Have and publish a marketing strategy/communication. 4. Meet capital requirements (show you have money — at least EUR 350,000). 5. Maintain reserves (at least 30% in segregated accounts, invested in low-risk instruments). 6. Draft and notify a doomsday/recovery and redemption plan to competent authorities.

entries.question: Under MiCA, what is the right of withdrawal period for retail holders who purchase crypto-assets?
Under MiCA, if a public crypto-asset offering is cancelled, within how many days must offerors return collected funds to holders?
Which was the first global stablecoin issuer to comply with MiCA, as noted in the slides?
Coinbase announced it would delist noncompliant stablecoins from its European platform under MiCA rules.
What are the research questions addressed in Academic Example 2 ('Raising the Bar')?
options: RQ1: Are regulators effective? RQ2: Is MiCA better than AMLD5?
RQ1: When left to self-regulate, did exchanges do a good job? RQ2: Compared to regulatory expectations, how good or bad were their practices?
RQ1: How many exchanges collapsed? RQ2: What caused the most collapses?
RQ1: Is MiCA enforceable? RQ2: Do VASPs comply with FATF?

entries.question: In the 'Raising the Bar' study (Academic Example 2), how many centralised exchanges in Europe were studied?
In the 'Raising the Bar' study, what percentage of exchanges satisfied above 40% of MiCA/DORA compliance criteria?
In the exchange liability study, what percentage of the 75 exchanges claimed they were 'Not Liable for Service Performance'?
In the exchange liability study, what percentage of exchanges disclaimed liability for cybercrime?
According to the 'Raising the Bar' study, exchanges that self-regulated demonstrated practices well-aligned with MiCA expectations.
What are the four main findings (gaps) identified in the 'Raising the Bar' academic study comparing exchange self-regulatory practices to MiCA/DORA frameworks?
entries.answer: 1. Self-Regulation Deficiencies: Ineffective internal controls. 2. Custodial Duty Gaps: Lack of clear protocols for asset protection. 3. Weak Security Policies: Insufficient safeguards against cyber threats and wallet security. 4. Liability Shift: Exchanges imposing risk on users through unfavourable contractual terms.

entries.question: What is the Bitcoin whitepaper and what is its relevance to the regulatory overview?
entries.back: The Bitcoin whitepaper, titled 'Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System,' was authored by Satoshi Nakamoto and published in 2008 during the global financial crisis. It proposed a purely peer-to-peer electronic cash system that would allow online payments without going through a trusted financial institution. It is relevant because Bitcoin was created in reaction to the failures of traditional banking but its lack of regulation led to its own set of problems.

entries.question: According to Academic Evidence 1, what percentage of cryptocurrency exchanges that existed before 2013 had collapsed by that point?
What challenge for regulators is described as: 'Nature of the technology is a challenge itself'?
Describe the five challenges regulators faced when trying to take action against cryptocurrency activities before 2023.
entries.answer: 1. New form of technology with no existing applicable standards. 2. No international consensus on what, how, and who to regulate. 3. No technical expert-regulators and no high-level understanding. 4. The nature of the technology (decentralisation, pseudonymous addresses, privacy coins, mixers) is itself a challenge. 5. Rapidly changing technology that outpaces regulatory efforts.

entries.question: The MiCA regulation is described as being like 'One Ring to Rule Them All' — but with what caveat?
options: It only applies in the Eurozone
It only covers exchanges and not stablecoin issuers
It covers almost all crypto-assets, with specific exclusions like NFTs, DeFi, and CBDCs

entries.question: Under MiCA, non-EU CASPs that seek to market their services to EU clients are not subject to MiCA requirements.
What is DORA in the context of the EU Digital Financial Package?
What was the regulatory significance of Tether being fined $41 million by the CFTC in October 2021?
entries.answer: The CFTC ordered Tether to pay $41 million over claims that Tether's USDT stablecoin was not fully backed by US dollars, despite representations to that effect. This demonstrated a real-world enforcement case against a stablecoin issuer for making untrue or misleading statements about its reserves — precisely the kind of reserve transparency and accountability that MiCA later sought to mandate through whitepaper requirements and reserve rules for EMT and ART issuers.

entries.question: Under MiCA's marketing requirements (Article 14), marketing communications must NOT include which of the following?
options: Marketing before the whitepaper has been published

entries.question: Under MiCA, competent authorities have the power to exercise control over marketing compliance of crypto-asset issuers.
What are the four goals of MiCA as outlined in its Overview and Scope?
entries.answer: 1. Consumer protection: rules covering trading, issuance, exchange, and custody of crypto-assets. 2. Authorisation and supervision: of crypto-asset service providers, issuers of e-money tokens, and issuers of asset-referenced tokens. 3. Uniform rules: for the issuance and trading of crypto-assets across the EU. 4. Market integrity: prevention and prohibition of market abuse and ensuring market integrity.

entries.question: The 'regulatory trial and error phase' before 2023 was characterised by which of the following?
options: Fragmented national regulations, no global collaboration, always changing, and no proper targeting of underlying risks

entries.question: Explain the key difference between AMLD5 and MiCA in their approach to regulating cryptocurrency transactions.
entries.answer: AMLD5 was the first EU legislation to address cryptocurrencies but it adapted existing AML/CFT standards (incorporating FATF Recommendations) to apply to exchanges. Critically, it only covered cryptocurrency-to-fiat transactions, leaving cryptocurrency-to-cryptocurrency transactions unregulated. MiCA, by contrast, establishes entirely new, specific standards for crypto-assets rather than adapting existing ones. It is far more comprehensive, covering exchange operations, token issuance (including stablecoins), consumer protection, market integrity, and liability — and it applies to a broader range of activities including crypto-to-crypto transactions within its scope.

entries.question: The quote 'Using new technology to break the law does not make you a disruptor, it makes you a criminal' was said by whom in reference to Binance?
FTX's Terms and Conditions explicitly stated that client funds should be segregated from the company's own funds.
What is a 'whitepaper' in the context of MiCA, and what must it contain for a crypto-asset offering?
entries.answer: A whitepaper is a mandatory disclosure document that crypto-asset issuers must draft, notify to relevant authorities before publication, and make publicly available. It must contain: (1) information on the underlying technology of the project and its characteristics; (2) rights and obligations of holders; (3) information about the issuer, the offeror, the offer to the public, associated risks, and environmental impact. The issuer must also provide a non-technical explanation of the whitepaper. In certain cases (e.g., targeting only qualified investors), the whitepaper does not need to be published.

entries.question: Which of the following best describes a 'Utility Token' under MiCA?
options: A token that maintains stable value by referencing one fiat currency
A token that provides access to a good or service supplied by its issuer
A token backed by real-world assets like gold
A token representing ownership in a company

entries.question: Under MiCA, CBDCs (Central Bank Digital Currencies) are subject to the same requirements as Electronic Money Tokens.
What methodology did the 'Raising the Bar' academic study use to evaluate exchange compliance?
options: A single survey sent to 75 exchanges
Three combined approaches: legal analysis of MiCA/DORA to create 85 compliance standards, empirical study of exchange documents (T&Cs, security policies), and compliance measurement comparing the two
Only quantitative analysis of exchange transaction data
Interviews with regulators and exchange operators

entries.question: What is the significance of the final statement from the Week 9 lecture: 'True power remains in proper and efficient enforcement of laws and regulations'?
entries.answer: This statement reflects a key theme throughout the lecture: even well-designed regulations are ineffective without enforcement. The history of cryptocurrency regulation shows that while frameworks like AMLD5, FATF Recommendations, and now MiCA have been established, actual compliance by exchanges has been very low (as evidenced by the 8% compliance finding in the academic study). Cases like Binance (which operated non-compliantly for years before being penalised) and FTX (which violated its own terms and conditions) show that having laws on paper is insufficient — regulators must actively investigate, prosecute, and penalise non-compliance for regulations to have real-world impact.
